title: Trauma and the Heart: How Emotional Wounds Affect Cardiovascular Health
excerpt: Explore how emotional trauma impacts heart health and learn somatic strategies to support cardiovascular resilience and healing.
body:
Understanding the Link Between Trauma and Heart Health

When we think of trauma, the physical consequences that often come to mind tend to focus on the brain or the nervous system. However, trauma doesn’t just live in the mind; it profoundly affects the body, especially the heart and cardiovascular system. Emotional wounds, particularly those left unaddressed, can alter heart function, blood pressure, and overall cardiovascular health. This connection is not just metaphorical—science now confirms that trauma and heart health are deeply intertwined.

In this article, we’ll explore the physiological mechanisms behind this link, drawing on the work of leading trauma researchers like Bessel van der Kolk, Stephen Porges, and Gabor Maté. We’ll also provide actionable insights into how somatic healing practices can support cardiovascular resilience.

How Does Trauma Impact the Cardiovascular System?

Trauma triggers a cascade of physiological responses designed for survival. The autonomic nervous system (ANS), which regulates involuntary bodily functions like heart rate and blood pressure, is central to this process. When trauma occurs, it can dysregulate the ANS, leading to chronic states of hyperarousal or shutdown.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a useful framework to understand this. Porges describes how the vagus nerve modulates heart rate variability (HRV), a key indicator of autonomic flexibility and cardiovascular health. Trauma can reduce HRV, meaning the heart becomes less adaptable to stress, increasing risk for hypertension, arrhythmias, and heart disease.

In practical terms, this means that trauma survivors often experience heightened heart rates, palpitations, or chest tightness without an obvious physical cause. These symptoms are not “just in your head” but are rooted in the body's trauma response system.

Chronic Stress, Inflammation, and Cardiovascular Disease

Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, highlights how trauma can embed itself in the body, leading to chronic stress. This ongoing stress activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, resulting in elevated cortisol levels. While cortisol is vital for short-term survival, prolonged elevation can cause systemic inflammation.

Inflammation is a well-known contributor to cardiovascular disease (CVD). Studies show that trauma survivors are at higher risk for CVD due to this chronic inflammatory state. Gabor Maté also emphasizes that early emotional wounds and adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) correlate strongly with later heart problems, largely because of the cumulative burden of stress and inflammation on the cardiovascular system.

Emotional Trauma and Heart Rate Variability (HRV)

Heart rate variability—the variation in time intervals between heartbeats—is a key biomarker for cardiovascular and emotional health. High HRV reflects a flexible, well-regulated nervous system, while low HRV is linked to stress, anxiety, depression, and heart disease.

Deb Dana, a clinical social worker and expert on Polyvagal Theory, explains that trauma can trap the nervous system in a state that lowers HRV, impairing the body’s ability to return to calm after stress. This diminished capacity not only affects emotional regulation but also increases the risk of cardiovascular complications.

Measuring and Improving HRV

  • Wearable devices like heart rate monitors can track HRV trends.
  • Somatic practices—such as breathwork, gentle movement, and mindfulness—can improve HRV by fostering autonomic balance.
  • Polyvagal-informed therapies focus on engaging the social engagement system to restore vagal tone and heart regulation.

Somatic Healing Approaches to Support Heart Health

Traditional talk therapy can fall short when addressing the embodied nature of trauma. Somatic healing techniques work directly with the body’s sensations and nervous system states to release trauma and promote regulation.

Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing helps clients track bodily sensations linked to trauma and complete incomplete fight, flight, or freeze responses. This process can reduce chronic activation of the heart and nervous system.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy

Mindful movement practices like yoga, qigong, and tai chi have been shown to reduce blood pressure and improve HRV by encouraging parasympathetic activation.

The Role of Attachment and Relational Safety

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model highlights how internal parts, often formed in response to trauma, affect emotional and physical health. When parts related to fear or shame dominate, they can perpetuate stress responses harmful to the heart.

Creating relational safety—whether through therapy, supportive relationships, or community—activates the parasympathetic nervous system and promotes cardiovascular health. Deb Dana emphasizes that social connection is a biological necessity, not a luxury, for regulating the heart and nervous system.

"Trauma doesn’t just live in the mind—it embeds itself in the rhythms of the heart and body, shaping health in profound ways." — The Oracle Lover

Practical Steps to Protect Your Heart from Trauma’s Impact

Recognizing the mind-body link in trauma is empowering. Here are practical steps to support cardiovascular health through somatic awareness:

  • Develop body awareness: Regularly check in with your bodily sensations related to stress and heart function.
  • Practice regulated breathing: Slow, diaphragmatic breathing stimulates the vagus nerve and enhances HRV.
  • Engage in gentle movement: Choose somatic exercises that feel safe and restorative.
  • Build safe relationships: Seek connection that feels supportive and grounding.
  • Consider trauma-informed therapy: Work with practitioners trained in somatic modalities to address trauma’s physical imprint.

Conclusion: Healing the Heart Requires Healing the Trauma

The emerging science on trauma and cardiovascular health underscores a simple truth: emotional wounds affect the heart as much as physical ones do. By integrating somatic healing methods that honor the body’s wisdom, individuals can reclaim heart health and resilience.

As Bessel van der Kolk states in The Body Keeps the Score, healing trauma is not just about talking—it’s about re-patterning the body’s responses and restoring balance to the nervous system and heart. The journey toward heart-centered healing is complex but deeply rewarding, offering a pathway to both emotional freedom and physical vitality.

For those who have felt stuck despite therapy, embracing somatic approaches can be a transformative next step in healing both trauma and the heart.

faqs.q: How does trauma physically affect the heart?
faqs.a: Trauma dysregulates the autonomic nervous system, leading to chronic stress responses like elevated heart rate and inflammation, which increase cardiovascular risk.

faqs.q: Can somatic healing improve heart health?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic practices that regulate the nervous system can enhance heart rate variability, reduce stress, and promote cardiovascular resilience.

faqs.q: What role does heart rate variability play in trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Heart rate variability reflects autonomic flexibility; improving HRV supports emotional regulation and reduces trauma’s impact on the heart.

faqs.q: Why is social connection important for heart health after trauma?
faqs.a: Relational safety activates the parasympathetic nervous system, calming the heart and nervous system, which is crucial for trauma recovery.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma affects the body and offers insights into somatic healing.

affiliateProducts.title: Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Introduces Somatic Experiencing, a powerful approach to releasing trauma held in the body.

affiliateProducts.title: Polyvagal Exercises for Safety and Connection by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: Provides practical exercises grounded in Polyvagal Theory to support nervous system regulation and heart health.
